coredata.dc:title: An electrochemical sensing platform for highly sensitive analysis of Sudan I based on Bi2S3/CQDs/GCE
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Suqin
Mei, Zhixiang
Li, Yanchao
Pan, Qilin
Dai, Gaopeng
Pan, Cong
coredata.dc:description: An electrochemical sensor that utilizes a composite of Bismuth sulfide (Bi2S3) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modified onto a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was employed to detect Sudan I, a synthetic dye. The Bi2S3 and CQDs were synthesized through a one-step hydrothermal process. The electrochemical behavior of Sudan I on the Bi2S3/CQDs/GCE was studied using Cyclic Voltammetry (CV). Experimental parameters such as the choice of supporting electrolyte, concentration, amount of drop-coating and ratio of Bi2S3 to CQDs were optimized to achieve the best detection performance. The results showed that the sensor has a wide linear detection range for Sudan I from 3.0 × 10−5 to 1.2 × 10−3 μM and a detection limit as low as 1.0 × 10−6 μM, with a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. The sensor demonstrated high sensitivity, with values of 475,621 and 151,624 μA.μM−1.cm−2, respectively. Additionally, the sensor exhibited good selectivity, reproducibility and stability, making it suitable for the determination of Sudan I in food products.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bismuth sulfide
CQDs
Sudan I
GCE
Electrochemical sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of supporting electrolyte and concentration
Electrochemical response of Sudan I at different electrodes
Stability, reproducibility and selectivity of the modified electrode

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Hubei Key Laboratory of Low Dimensional Optoelectronic Material and Devices
China Petroleum and Chemical Industry Federation

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51508172, 51572076 and 51378183), China Petroleum and Chemical Industry Federation and Hubei Key Laboratory of Low Dimensional Optoelectronic Material and Devices (HLOM231003).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a-b) SEM of as-synthesized Bi2S3, (c) XRD spectra, (d) Raman diagram and (e) Infrared spectrum of CQDs, Bi2S3, Bi2S3/CQDs complex.
(a) SEM image, (b-e) mapping and EDS of Bi2S3 and CQDs complexes.

(a) The cyclic voltammograms at bare GCE, CQDs/GCE, Bi2S3/GCE and Bi2S3/CQDs/GCE in 0.2 mol/L KOH with a scan rate of 100 mV/s; (b) Nyquist plots of bare GCE, CQDs/GCE, Bi2S3/GCE and Bi2S3/CQDs/GCE in 0.1 M KCl with 4 mM [Fe(CN)6]4−/3−; (c) Plots of peak currents vs. square root of scan rates (10–500 mV/s) derived from partial CV curves (inset B) and full CV chart at 10 mV/s (inset A) for Bi2S3/CQDs/GCE in 0.1 M KCl with 4 mM [Fe(CN)6]4−/3−.
(a) Effect of KOH solution concentration, (b) CQDs mass of complex and (c) dropping amounts, respectively.

Comparison CVs of (a) Bi2S3/CQDs/GCE, (b) bare GCE, (c) CQDs/GCE and (d) Bi2S3/GCE in the absence and presence of 0.4 nM Sudan I. (e) CVs of Bi2S3/CQDs/GCE with various Sudan I concentrations; (f) the relationship diagram of peak currents I pc vs. Sudan I concentration. (g) I pc responses at five independently Bi2S3/CQDs/GCE to 0.4 nM Sudan I and (h) anti-interference test of Bi2S3/CQDs/GCE in the determination of 0.4 nM Sudan I in the presence of some interferents with 100-foldes.
Illustration of Bi2S3/CQDs/GCE for Sudan I detection.
Comparison of the electrochemical performances used for Sudan I detection.
Determination of Sudan I detection in chili powder of Bi2S3/CQDs/GCE (n = 3).

head.ce:title: An electrochemical sensing platform for highly sensitive analysis of Sudan I based on Bi2S3/CQDs/GCE

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food Science and Chemical Engineering
Hubei University of Arts and Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An electrochemical sensor that utilizes a composite of Bismuth sulfide (Bi2S3) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modified onto a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was employed to detect Sudan I, a synthetic dye. The Bi2S3 and CQDs were synthesized through a one-step hydrothermal process. The electrochemical behavior of Sudan I on the Bi2S3/CQDs/GCE was studied using Cyclic Voltammetry (CV). Experimental parameters such as the choice of supporting electrolyte, concentration, amount of drop-coating and ratio of Bi2S3 to CQDs were optimized to achieve the best detection performance. The results showed that the sensor has a wide linear detection range for Sudan I from 3.0 × 10−5 to 1.2 × 10−3 μM and a detection limit as low as 1.0 × 10−6 μM, with a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. The sensor demonstrated high sensitivity, with values of 475,621 and 151,624 μA.μM−1.cm−2, respectively. Additionally, the sensor exhibited good selectivity, reproducibility and stability, making it suitable for the determination of Sudan I in food products.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Sudan I, chemically known as 1-phenylazo-2-naphthol, is a synthetic lipophilic azo compound and one of the red pigments in the Sudan Red series [1–2]. It is primarily utilized as a coloring agent. On October 27, 2017, the International Agency for Research on Cancer of the World Health Organization classified Sudan I as a Group 3 carcinogen [3]. As a result, many countries have explicitly banned its use as a food additive. Despite this, some dishonest vendors continue to use Sudan I in food products, such as chili peppers and pickles, due to its vibrant color. This scenario highlights the urgent need for a detection method that is sensitive, reliable, rapid, and cost-effective. Existing methods for detecting Sudan I include high-performance liquid chromatography [4], fluorescence [5], Thin-Layer chromatography surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy [6], Near-Infrared spectroscopy [7], and 1H-nuclear magnetic resonance [8]. However, these methods either involve time-consuming pre-treatment, complex experimental conditions, or expensive and large-scale instrumentation, which limits their widespread application.

In contrast, electrochemical analytical methods have recently risen to prominence as a research focal point in the identification of biological and environmental analytes, primarily due to their remarkable sensitivity, robustness, simplicity, speed, and precision. Within the domain of biological analysis, electrochemical techniques exhibit particular efficacy in detecting biomolecules, including pharmaceuticals [9], glucose, cholesterol, DNA, and small metabolites such as hydrogen peroxide. Additionally, these methods facilitate the investigation of electron transfer processes within proteins on electrode surfaces, thereby enabling the detection of enzyme activity or concentration. Electrochemical approaches are extensively employed in the detection of alterations in neurotransmitters (e.g., dopamine, serotonin) and metabolites (e.g., lactic acid, uric acid). These sensors commonly utilize microelectrode technology to facilitate real-time monitoring in live organisms, including humans.

Furthermore, in the field of environmental analysis, electrochemical sensors demonstrate high sensitivity in detecting heavy metal ions within the environment, including lead (Pb2+), cadmium (Cd2+), and mercury (Hg2+) [10]. These sensors are also utilized for the detection of water pollutants such as phenols, nitrates, nitrites, pesticide residues, and organic contaminants [11–12]. Electrochemical gas sensors are extensively employed for detecting toxic and hazardous gases in the environment, including carbon monoxide (CO), sulfur dioxide (SO2), and nitrogen oxides (NOx). The real-time capability of these methods supports swift decision-making in response to environmental threats, thereby significantly enhancing public health and safety.

In the field of food safety and quality control, electrochemical sensors have been garnered significant attention [13]. Nevertheless, the sensitivity of electrochemical sensors is often constrained by low electron transfer rates and redox reaction rates, thus development of a more sensitive sensor is a significant challenge. The integration of electroanalytical methods with nanotechnology has further expanded their capabilities. Nanomaterials, such as carbon materials and transition metal compounds, can enhance the electrochemical signals, leading to improved detection limits and faster response times [2,14]. Electrochemical reduction properties of Sudan I make electrochemical methods particularly suitable for its detection [14–16].

Bi2S3 is a semiconductor material known for its narrow bandgap and extensive visible light absorption capabilities. Its advantageous photoelectric and thermoelectric properties have led to its widespread use in photoelectric sensors and catalytic processes [17]. Bi2S3 has also been explored for various applications, including food preservation, detection of aflatoxin B1, and identification of antibiotic residues [18]. For instance, Kogularasu et al. successfully synthesized sea urchin-like Bi2S3 nanorods via a hydrothermal method and created a sensing platform for real-time detection of NO2 − by modifying a glassy carbon electrode with g-C3N4 [19]. Moreover, Wu et al. developed a Z-type heterojunction Bi2S3/Bi2O2CO3 to construct a molecularly imprinted photoelectrochemical sensor specifically for detecting aflatoxin B1 [20]. This sensor demonstrated an impressive linear detection range from 0.01 to 1000 ng/mL and achieved a low detection limit of 2.95 pg/mL. However, to date, there has been minimal research dedicated to the modification of electrodes with Bi2S3 for the sensitive detection of Sudan I.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are emerging zero-dimensional nanocarbon materials characterized by their dispersed, spherical-like carbon particles with extremely small dimensions, typically less than or equal to 10 nm, and possess intrinsic fluorescent properties. These nanomaterials generally feature a combination of amorphous and crystalline carbon cores, which are coated with various functional groups such as hydroxyl (–OH) and carboxyl (–COOH) groups. The exceptional photoelectronic properties of CQDs, coupled with their cost-effective synthesis, low toxicity, and environmental friendliness, make them highly attractive for various applications [21]. In recent years, CQDs have attracted considerable interest across a range of fields due to their unique characteristics. In bioimaging, they are used as fluorescent probes to enhance imaging contrast and resolution, enabling more precise biological observations. For drug delivery, CQDs can be engineered to facilitate targeted delivery and controlled release of therapeutic agents, thereby improving treatment efficacy. Additionally, CQDs have shown promise in enhancing fingerprint recognition systems due to their fluorescence properties, which can improve the clarity and accuracy of fingerprint imaging. In chemical biosensors, CQDs are employed to develop sensitive and selective detection systems for various analytes, leveraging their optical properties for improved sensor performance. Lastly, their catalytic capabilities are being explored in various chemical reactions, where they can act as effective catalysts, potentially leading to more efficient and environmentally friendly chemical processes [22–24]. Overall, the versatility and innovative potential of CQDs make them a valuable subject of research and development across numerous scientific and industrial domains.

Numerous studies have highlighted the use of CQDs for the detection of Sudan I. For instance, Yang et al reported the use of sulfur-doped carbon quantum dots derived from lignin as a sensor for detecting Sudan I in acidic environments [25]. The SCQDs proved effective as a sensor for Sudan I in acidic solutions, offering excellent selectivity and sensitivity, with a linear detection range of 0–40 μM and a limit of detection of 0.12 μM. Additionally, multifunctional blue-fluorescent carbon dots (B-CDs) were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal process using glucose as the carbon source. The fluorescence intensity of B-CDs is quenched by Sudan I, and the fluorescence quenching intensity exhibits a robust linear relationship with the concentration of Sudan I (0.088–65 µM) across pH levels of 3–7. This method has been successfully applied to real food samples, with a detection limit of 26.3 nM [5]. Furthermore, a fluorescence probe based on CQDs was developed for Sudan I determination, utilizing cigarette filters as the carbon source. A linear relationship was observed within the range of 2.40–104.0 μmol/L Sudan I, with a detection limit (3σ/k) of 0.95 μmol/L [26]. In summary, studies employing CQDs for the detection of Sudan I have predominantly focused on fluorescent sensors with high sensitivity and a wide linear range. Notably, the application of electrochemical analysis methods based on CQDs-modified electrodes for Sudan I detection has not been reported.

Taking advantage of the exceptional properties of both Bi2S3 and CQDs, this thesis focuses on synthesizing nano-hollow rods of Bi2S3 and nano-CQDs using a hydrothermal method. Following the synthesis, the thesis involves the combination of these two materials to create Bi2S3/CQDs-modified glassy carbon electrodes (Bi2S3/CQDs/GCE). The research endeavors to enhance the sensitivity and specificity of electrochemical detection methods for Sudan I, marking a pioneering contribution to analytical techniques and potentially improving the monitoring of this compound across diverse applications. The detailed and visualized process of constructing the Bi2S3/CQDs/GCE for Sudan I detection is illustrated in Scheme 1 of the thesis. The findings demonstrated outstanding selectivity and sensitivity of the Bi2S3/CQDs/GCE towards Sudan I, featuring a broad linear range (3.0 × 10−5-1.2 × 10−3 μM), high sensitivity ((475621 and 151,624 μA.μM−1.cm−2), a low limit of detection (LOD) of 1.0 × 10–6 μM, exceptional reproducibility, and acceptable stability.

Sodium thiosulfate, bismuth nitrate, sodium sulfide, urea, Sudan I, Sudan II (SD II), Sudan III (SD III), Sudan IV (SD IV), azobenzene (AZB), lysine and ascorbic acid were of analytically pure grade and were acquired from MacklinBiochemicalCo.,Ltd (Shanghai, China). All other chemicals utilized in the study were purchased from Tianjin Yongsheng Chemical Co., Ltd. (Tianjin,

China) of analytical grade and used as received, without any further purification or processing. For the preparation of all aqueous solutions, deionized water was employed, which was obtained from a Millipore water purification system with a resistivity of ≥ 18 MΩ cm (Milli-Q, Millipore, Yixin Scientific Instrument Co., Shanghai).

Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) and Energy-Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDS) characterizations were performed using an S-4800 field-emission scanning electron microscope manufactured by Hitachi, Japan. Infrared absorption spectra were acquired using a Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer model IRPrestige-21 from SHIMADZU, Japan. X-ray Diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded with a D8 ADVANCE X-ray diffractometer from Bruker AXS Co., Germany. The graphitization degree of CQDs, Bi2S3 and Bi2S3/CQDs were identified by a Raman DXR spectrometer (Thermo, USA) with a 532 nm laser. Electrochemical data were gathered using a CHI660E electrochemical workstation provided by Shanghai Chenhua Co., China. For electrochemical measurements, a three-electrode system was utilized. This system comprised a bare or modified glassy carbon electrode, which served as the working electrode and had a diameter of 2 mm, a saturated calomel electrode used as the reference electrode, and a platinum wire electrode functioning as the auxiliary electrode.

0.73 g of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O were dissolved in 5 mL of ethylene glycol and stirred for 5 min to yield a clear solution, which was designated as Solution A. In a separate process, 1.5 g of sodium sulfide Na2S were dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water to prepare Solution B. Solution B was then added dropwise to Solution A while continuously and rapidly stirring the mixture until a black precursor was formed. Following this, 0.76 g of urea CO(NH2)2 were introduced to the black precursor. The resulting mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave, where it underwent hydrothermal treatment at 120 °C for 12 h. After the hydrothermal treatment, the product was washed sequentially with deionized water and anhydrous ethanol. The washed product was then dried under vacuum at 80 °C for 6 h and set aside for further use.

0.75 g of sucrose were dissolved in 30 mL of deionized water and stirred for 30 min to ensure complete dissolution. This solution was then transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave for hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 5 h. After the hydrothermal process, the autoclave was allowed to cool to room temperature. The resulting solution was then subjected to centrifugation at 8000 revolutions per minute for 20 min to separate the solid particles from the liquid. The supernatant obtained from this centrifugation was collected and transferred to a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cutoff of 1 kDa. The solution within the dialysis bag was purified through dialysis for 24 h to remove any small impurities or unwanted substances. Finally, the purified solution was subjected to freeze-drying to remove the remaining water content, resulting in a dry product that was prepared for further use.

The bare glassy carbon electrode, with a diameter of 2 mm, was first polished using aluminum Al2O3 with a particle size of 0.05 μm to achieve a smooth surface. After polishing, the electrode was ultrasonically cleaned in anhydrous ethanol followed by deionized water to ensure the removal of any residual contaminants, until the surface became mirror-smooth. The cleaned electrode was then dried with N2. A mixture was prepared by dissolving 9.5 mg of Bi2S3 powder and 0.5 mg of CQDs powder in 10 mL of ethanol. This solution was thoroughly sonicated to ensure complete dispersion of the powders. A suitable amount of this well-dispersed mixture was then drop-coated onto the surface of the treated glassy carbon electrodes. The electrodes were left to air-dry, and these were then designated as Bi2S3/CQDs/GCE. For comparison purposes, Bi2S3/GCE and CQDs/GCE were prepared using the same procedure, but with Bi2S3 and CQDs applied separately.

Two portions of chili powder were purchased from different local supermarkets. Each portion was 1 g in weight. The 1 g samples of chili powder from each source were individually dispersed in ethanol by subjecting them to ultrasonication for 30 min. Following ultrasonication, the mixture was filtered through a 0.45 μm polytetrafluoroethylene syringe filter membrane to remove any solid particles. The resulting supernatant was collected and transferred to a volumetric flask for further processing. In addition, a specified amount of Sudan I standard solution was added to a separate 1 g sample of chili powder. This sample was then processed using the same ultrasonication, filtration, and collection steps described above. The processed sample, now containing the Sudan I standard, was designated as a simulated sample.

Fig. 1 a displays the microscopic morphology of the Bi2S3 nanomaterial, as observed using SEM. The image reveals that Bi2S3 is composed of nanorods, some of which exhibit aggregation. Fig. 1b provides a magnified view of the Bi2S3 nanorods, showing a relatively regular nanorod-like structure with a uniform morphology and hollow centers. Each nanorod measures between 2 µm and 4 µm in length and between 100 nm and 200 nm in width. Fig. 1c presents the XRD patterns of pure Bi2S3 and Bi2S3/CQDs composites. For pure Bi2S3, all observed diffraction peaks correspond to the orthorhombic Bi2S3 structure, as referenced by JCPDS Card No. 00-017-0320 [27]. Notable diffraction peaks are observed from the (020), (130), (211) and (221) planes. In the XRD pattern of the Bi2S3/CQDs composites, only the peaks corresponding to the orthorhombic Bi2S3 phase are detected, with no discernible peaks from the CQDs. This lack of CQD peaks may be attributed to the high dispersion of CQDs, their poor crystallinity, or their concentration being below the detection limit of XRD. Additionally, no significant shifts in peak positions or peak broadening were observed, indicating that the incorporation of CQDs did not alter the physical structure of Bi2S3.

Fig. 1d presents the Raman spectra of bulk Bi2S3 powder, bulk CQDs powder, and Bi2S3/CQDs composites. The Raman spectrum of the as-synthesized Bi2S3 powder reveals five distinct peaks at wavenumbers of 103 cm−1, 200 cm−1, 226 cm−1, 420 cm−1 and 966 cm−1 within the range of 100–3000 cm−1. These peaks are consistent with the characteristic features of Bi2S3 nanocrystals as reported by [28]. For the CQDs, the spectrum displays two prominent peaks at approximately 1375 cm−1 and 1576 cm−1, which correspond to the D-band and G-band of carbon, respectively. The peak at 1375 cm−1 (D-band) is attributed to the vibrations of sp3-bonded carbon atoms within disordered graphite, while the peak at 1576 cm−1 (G-band) is associated with the vibrations of sp2 carbon atoms in a two-dimensional hexagonal lattice structure, as detailed by [29]. In the Raman spectrum of the Bi2S3/CQDs composite, both the characteristic peaks of Bi2S3 and the D and G bands of CQDs are observable at 1375 cm−1 and 1576 cm−1, respectively. This confirms the presence of both Bi2S3 and CQDs within the composite. Notably, the Bi2S3/CQDs composite exhibits an increased ID/IG ratio, rising from 0.83 to 0.91, which suggests a reduction in the average size of the sp2 domains in the CQDs compared to the pure CQDs. Fig. 1e shows the FT-IR spectra of pure Bi2S3, pure CQDs and Bi2S3/CQDs composites. The FT-IR spectrum of pure Bi2S3 shows no absorption peaks within the range of 4000–400 cm−1, which is consistent with the results reported by [30]. For pure CQDs, a broad peak around 3500 cm−1 is observed, attributed to O-H stretching vibrations. Additional absorption bands are present at 1704 cm−1 and 1625 cm−1, corresponding to CO stretching and CC stretching vibrations, respectively, as reported by [31]. Peaks at 783 cm−1 and 1028 cm−1 are assigned to δ(C-H) and C-C stretching vibrations, respectively. In the FT-IR spectrum of the Bi2S3/CQDs composite, the vibrational features of CQDs are still evident, albeit with some shifts due to the presence of Bi2S3. This indicates that the integration of CQDs with Bi2S3 has been successfully achieved.

The chemical composition of the Bi2S3/CQDs composite was thoroughly analyzed using SEM in conjunction with EDS and mapping techniques, as depicted in Fig. 2 . The SEM-EDS analysis revealed strong signals corresponding to the elements Bi, S, C and O, thereby confirming the presence of these elements in the composite material. Among these elements, Bi exhibited the highest signal intensity, indicating its predominant presence, followed by S, with C and O showing comparatively lower signal intensities. These results align with the expected elemental composition of the Bi2S3/CQDs composite, confirming the successful synthesis of the material with a relatively small quantity of CQDs. The observed elemental distribution and relative abundances are consistent with the intended sample composition, verifying that the Bi2S3/CQDs composite was prepared as designed.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was utilized to investigate the electrochemical behavior of the bare GCE, CQDs/GCE, Bi2S3/GCE and Bi2S3/CQDs/GCE within a three-electrode system. The 0.2 mol/L KOH solution served as the electrolyte for this experiment. The scanning rate was set at 100 mV/s, and the potential range was limited from −0.1 V to 0 V, as illustrated in Fig. 3 a. The bare glassy carbon electrode exhibited a negligible presence of redox peaks, indicating minimal electrochemical activity. The GCE modified with CQDs/GCE displayed a faint oxidation peak, suggesting a slight improvement in electrochemical properties. However, the GCE modified with Bi2S3/GCE and the GCE modified with a combination of Bi2S3/CQDs/GCE showed significant redox peaks, demonstrating enhanced electrochemical behaviors. Interestingly, bismuth sulfide in an alkaline electrolyte, as in the case of Bi2S3/GCE and Bi2S3/CQDs/GCE, exhibited pseudocapacitive behavior. During the reaction, Bi3+ ions were oxidized to Bi5+ ions, and the reduction process was found to be reversible, as reported by [32]. Notably, the Bi2S3/CQDs/GCE electrode exhibited the most substantial redox peak current, which was approximately 1.5 times greater than that observed in the case of Bi2S3/GCE. This significant enhancement in electrochemical behavior can be attributed to the remarkable properties of CQDs. These properties include high electrical conductivity and a large active surface area, which can be efficiently tailored for specific applications. The incorporation of CQDs with Bi2S3 not only serves as anchoring sites for heteroatoms but also provides excellent electrical conductivity, as pointed out by [33]. This combination of CQDs and Bi2S3 opens up new avenues for enhancing electrocatalytic properties and electron transfer rates, promising significant improvements in the field of electrochemistry and beyond.

The electrochemical performance of the catalyst-modified electrodes was assessed using CV at varying scan rates in a 0.1 M KCl solution containing 4 mM [Fe(CN)6]4−/3− as a redox probe, aimed at understanding the charge transfer capabilities and determining the electrochemically active surface area. Fig. 3b presents the Nyquist plot generated from electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests, which were conducted to evaluate the charge transfer capability of the modified electrodes. Each modified electrode exhibited a semicircle shape followed by consecutive straight lines, with the conductivity of the electrode dependent on the diameter of the semicircle in the EIS curve. The semicircle diameters of the electrodes were in the order: bare GCE > Bi2S3/GCE > CQDs/GCE > Bi2S3/CQDs/GCE. Consequently, Bi2S3/CQDs/GCE displayed the smallest diameter, indicating its superior electron transfer ability and enhanced conductivity during the electrochemical reaction.

And the electrochemically active surface area of Bi2S3/CQDs/GCE was investigated and presented in Fig. 3c. Inset curve A shows the CV of Bi2S3/CQDs/GCE in a 0.1 M KCl solution containing 4 mM [Fe(CN)6]4−/3− over a potential range of −1.0 to 1.0 V. The CV curves display three pairs of redox peaks, with one pair at 0.13–0.25 V corresponding to the [Fe(CN)6]3−/[Fe(CN)6]4− redox probe. Another pair of peaks at 0 V and −0.48 V corresponds to the H+/H2 redox probe, and the remaining pair is attributed to the redox process of Bi5+/Bi3+. Inset curve B presents a partial cyclic voltammogram of Bi2S3/CQDs/GCE in 0.1 M KCl with 4 mM [Fe(CN)6]4−/3−, measured over a potential range of 0.1 to 1.0 V according to the [Fe(CN)6]3−/[Fe(CN)6]4− redox probe. Fig. 3c indicates that the oxidation current (Ip) is proportional to the square roots of the scan rates (ν1/2, 10–500 mV/s), with I p(μA) = 2.8616ν1/2 + 6.6201, R2 = 0.9981. As documented in the literature, applying the Randles–Sevcik equation, I p = 2.69 × 105 AD1/2n3/2ν1/2C, where the number of transferred electrons (n) is 1 and the diffusion coefficient (D) is 7.60 × 10−6 cm2 s−1 for [Fe(CN)6]4−/3−, the geometric surface area (A) is directly proportional to the slope of the peak current versus the square root of the scan rate [34]. Therefore, the electroactive surface area (A) was calculated to be 0.1702 cm2, approximately five times greater than that of bare GCE (0.0314 cm2), substantiating the exceptional electrochemical performance attributed to the enlarged electroactive surface area due to the incorporation of CQDs with Bi2S3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of supporting electrolyte and concentration
ce:section.ce:para: We sought to understand the reasons behind the optimal performance of the 0.2 M KOH solution (Fig. 4 a). Firstly, the ionic conductivity of KOH is higher compared to other electrolytes, facilitating efficient charge transfer within the electrochemical system. This enhanced conductivity likely contributes to the improved peak current observed with the 0.2 M KOH solution. Moreover, the alkaline environment created by the KOH solution may favor the electrochemical reactions occurring at the surface of the Bi2S3/CQDs/GCE electrode. The presence of hydroxyl ions could potentially catalyze the redox reactions, leading to a more pronounced electrochemical response. Additionally, the optimal concentration of 0.2 M KOH might strike a balance between the availability of ions for charge transfer and the prevention of excessive ionic strength that could otherwise hinder the electrochemical processes. At higher concentrations, the increased viscosity and ionic strength of the solution may slow down the diffusion of analyte molecules to the electrode surface, resulting in a decreased peak current and distorted peak shape. Furthermore, the interaction between the KOH electrolyte and the surface of the Bi2S3/CQDs/GCE could also play a role in the observed optimal performance. The specific adsorption of ions or the formation of an electrically favorable double layer at the electrode–electrolyte interface might enhance the electrochemical activity. In conclusion, the selection of 0.2 M KOH as the optimal supporting electrolyte was based on its ability to enhance the electronic conductivity of Bi2S3/CQDs/GCE, catalyze electrochemical reactions, and maintain an optimal ionic environment for efficient charge transfer. These factors collectively contributed to the improved sensing signal observed with the Bi2S3/CQDs/GCE electrode in the presence of 0.2 M KOH.

As illustrated in Fig. 4b, the electrochemical behavior of the Bi2S3/CQDs/GCE was examined using CV with varying percentages of CQDs (0 %, 0.5 %, 1 %, and 2 %). The study revealed that as the CQDs content increased, the electrochemical signal response improved incrementally, with the response peaking at 0.5 % CQDs. However, when the CQDs concentration was further increased beyond this point, the response began to diminish. The figure clearly demonstrates that the Bi2S3/CQDs/GCE containing 0.5 % CQDs exhibits the most pronounced peak current and the most defined peak shape compared to other concentrations. This optimal performance at 0.5 % CQDs indicates that this concentration provides the best balance between enhancing electrochemical activity and maintaining effective signal resolution. Based on these observations, the Bi2S3/CQDs/GCE composite with 0.5 % CQDs was determined to be the most effective formulation for the modified electrode in this experiment. This concentration was chosen as the optimal composition, maximizing both the peak current and the quality of the electrochemical response.

The experiment demonstrated that varying the drop-coating volumes significantly affected the electrochemical behavior of the modified electrode (Fig. 4c). With a 0.5 µL coating volume, the electrode exhibited suboptimal peak shapes and lower peak currents, suggesting insufficient coverage of the electrode surface. Increasing the coating volume to 2 µL resulted in broader peak shapes and a decrease in peak current, which might be attributed to excessive coating leading to possible aggregation or non-uniform distribution of the composite material. The optimal performance achieved with a 1 µL drop-coating volume indicates a well-balanced coverage that ensures effective interaction between the electrode surface and the analyte, maximizing the electrochemical response. This volume likely provides a sufficient amount of the composite solution to form a stable and uniform film on the electrode surface, facilitating enhanced electron transfer and improved sensing capabilities. Therefore, the findings underscore the importance of precise control over the coating volume in electrode modification processes, as it directly influences the electrochemical properties and overall performance of the electrode. The established optimal volume of 1 µL serves as a guideline for achieving the best results in similar electrochemical applications using the drop-coating method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical response of Sudan I at different electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 a-d present the CV curves for Sudan I (0.4 nM) using different electrode configurations: Bi2S3/CQDs/GCE, bare glassy carbon electrode (GCE), CQDs-modified GCE, and Bi2S3-modified GCE, all assessed in a 0.2 M KOH solution. The CV curves reveal weak electrochemical signals for both the bare GCE (Fig. 5b) and the CQDs-modified GCE (Fig. 5c), regardless of the presence of Sudan I. This indicates that these electrodes exhibit limited electrochemical activity for Sudan I detection. In contrast, the Bi2S3-modified GCE (Fig. 5d) shows a well-defined redox pair, with Sudan I resulting in a significantly higher redox current compared to both the unmodified and CQDs-modified electrodes. Notably, the Bi2S3/CQDs/GCE prepared with 0.5 % CQDs, as depicted in Fig. 5a, demonstrates a substantial enhancement in the response signal. This improvement can be attributed to the exceptional electrical conductivity of CQDs, which facilitates more efficient electron transfer, and their large specific surface area, which offers increased adhesion sites for Sudan I molecules. This combination of factors results in a notably increased response current. The sharply increased redox peak current observed for the Bi2S3/CQDs/GCE indicates that this electrode exhibits excellent electron-transfer capabilities and adsorption properties. This enhanced performance underscores the superior effectiveness of the Bi2S3/CQDs/GCE in detecting Sudan I compared to the other electrode configurations tested. Overall, the Bi2S3/CQDs/GCE proves to be the most effective in Sudan I detection, showcasing its superior analytical performance.

Under the optimized experimental conditions, CV was employed to determine the concentration of Sudan I in a 0.2 M KOH solution using the Bi2S3/CQDs/GCE electrode. As shown in Fig. 5e, the redox peak current increased progressively with the rising concentration of Sudan I. Specifically, the concentration of Sudan I ranging from 3.0 × 10−5 μM to 1.2 × 10−3 μM was found to be proportional to I pc, following the equations: I pc(μA) = -0.6678 + 59780.3C (μM, R = 0.9973, C = 3.0 × 10−5 ∼ 3.0 × 10−4 μM), and I pc(μA) = 12.57 + 19041.3C (μM, R = 0.9938, C = 3.5 × 10−4 ∼ 1.2 × 10−3 μM), as depicted in Fig. 5f. The LOD for Sudan I was calculated to be 1.0 × 10−6 μM, with a signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) of 3. The sensor proposed in this study enables ultrasensitive electrochemical analysis of Sudan I, with calculated sensitivities of 475,621 and 151,624 μA.μM−1.cm−2, respectively. In summary, the Sudan I sensor developed and described in this paper demonstrates high sensitivity, a notably low detection limit, and a broad detection range. It significantly outperforms other electrochemical sensors for Sudan I detection reported in previous studies, as detailed in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability, reproducibility and selectivity of the modified electrode
ce:section.ce:para: The Bi2S3/CQDs/GCE electrode was carefully stored in dry air to preserve its performance over time. To evaluate its stability, the modified electrode was used to measure a 0.4 nM Sudan I solution every 5 days. After each measurement, the electrode was regenerated to its initial state through CV scanning in a blank KOH solution before being stored in air again. After 5 days of storage, the electrode retained 98.52 % of its initial current value. Over a period of 15 days, the current value showed a minor decrease of only 4.67 %. Although the current value continued to decline slightly with extended storage time, this gradual decrease indicates that the Bi2S3/CQDs/GCE electrode exhibits excellent stability over time (Fig. 5g). Reproducibility, an essential aspect for reliable measurements and analysis, was also rigorously evaluated. To test reproducibility, the same electrode was subjected to 30 continuous scanning cycles with a 0.4 nM Sudan I solution. The peak currents were recorded throughout these cycles, yielding a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 2.75 %. Furthermore, to assess the consistency between different electrodes, five identical Bi2S3/CQDs/GCE electrodes were prepared, and their electrochemical responses to 0.4 nM Sudan I were measured using CV. The RSD of the peak currents from these electrodes was 2.78 %, demonstrating good reproducibility and consistency in performance. In addition to stability and reproducibility, the selectivity of the newly developed sensor for detecting Sudan I was thoroughly assessed. This involved testing for potential interference from Sudan analogues, including Sudan II SDII, SDIII, SDIV and AZB, as well as common chili ingredients like lysine, ascorbic acid, and various ions. These interferents were tested at concentrations 100 times higher than the 0.4 nM concentration of Sudan I. The results, illustrated in Fig. 5h, showed that none of the tested interferents had a significant impact on the Sudan I sensing signal. In summary, the Bi2S3/CQDs/GCE electrode demonstrated outstanding stability, reproducibility, and selectivity, confirming its efficacy as a reliable and precise sensor for Sudan I detection.

To thoroughly evaluate the practical applicability of the modified electrode, it was employed in a series of experiments to detect the presence of Sudan I in chili powders procured from two distinct supermarkets. The detection process utilized the standard addition method, a rigorous approach designed to enhance the accuracy and precision of analytical measurements by adding known quantities of the analyte to the samples. The results of these experiments, which are comprehensively presented in Table 2 , showcased commendable recovery rates, ranging from 98.6 % to 104.2 %. These recovery rates are indicative of the method's high reliability and accuracy in quantifying Sudan I in complex matrices like chili powders. The observed recovery rates fall within the acceptable range, further validating the effectiveness of the Bi2S3/CQDs/GCE electrochemical sensor. This sensor proved to be not only effective in detecting Sudan I but also demonstrated its robustness and applicability in real sample analysis. The satisfactory results confirm that the Bi2S3/CQDs/GCE sensor is well-suited for practical use in food safety assessments, ensuring that it can be reliably employed for monitoring and controlling the presence of Sudan I in various food products. This successful validation highlights the sensor's potential for broader applications in ensuring food safety and quality, providing a reliable tool for regulatory and quality control purposes in the food industry.

A novel analytical method was developed to detect Sudan I by modifying the surface of a GCE with a composite of Bi2S3 and CQDs through the use of CV. This new sensor demonstrated exceptional sensitivity towards Sudan I detection. A clear linear relationship was established between the CV response of the modified electrode and the concentration of Sudan I, which ranged from 3.0 × 10−5 to 1.2 × 10−3 μM. The LOD for Sudan I was determined to be 1.0 × 10−6 μM, indicating a high level of detection capability. Moreover, the Bi2S3/CQDs/GCE sensor showed remarkable stability, reproducibility, and selectivity when detecting as low as 0.4 nM of Sudan I. The sensor's performance was further validated by testing chili powder samples, which resulted in acceptable recovery rates. This confirms that the Bi2S3/CQDs/GCE sensor is highly effective and suitable for various analytical applications involving Sudan I detection.

Suqin Liu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation. Zhixiang Mei: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Formal analysis. Yanchao Li: Software, Methodology. Qilin Pan: Software, Methodology. Gaopeng Dai: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Data curation. Cong Pan: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51508172, 51572076 and 51378183), China Petroleum and Chemical Industry Federation and Hubei Key Laboratory of Low Dimensional Optoelectronic Material and Devices (HLOM231003).
